(English: Romanian Cup) is a [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") cup competition for [Romanian](/wiki/List_of_football_clubs_in_Romania "List of football clubs in Romania") teams which has been held annually since [1933–34](/wiki/1933%E2%80%9334_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "1933–34 Cupa României"), except during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). It is the [country](/wiki/Romania "Romania")'s main cup competition and is open to all clubs affiliated with the [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation") (FRF) and the county football associations, regardless of the league in which they compete. The winner of the competition qualifies for the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") qualifying rounds and faces the league champion in the [Supercupa României](/wiki/Supercupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "Supercupa României").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Romanian association football tournament

For other sports, see [Cupa României (disambiguation)](/wiki/Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei_(disambiguation) "Cupa României (disambiguation)").

Football tournament

Cupa României

The **Cupa României** (English: Romanian Cup) is a [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") cup competition for [Romanian](/wiki/List_of_football_clubs_in_Romania "List of football clubs in Romania") teams which has been held annually since [1933–34](/wiki/1933%E2%80%9334_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "1933–34 Cupa României"), except during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). It is the [country](/wiki/Romania "Romania")'s main cup competition and is open to all clubs affiliated with the [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation") (FRF) and the county football associations, regardless of the league in which they compete. The winner of the competition qualifies for the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") qualifying rounds and faces the league champion in the [Supercupa României](/wiki/Supercupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "Supercupa României").

The Romanian Cup is widely known for its knockout format and the possibility of surprising results, with lower-division teams occasionally eliminating top-flight clubs. Over the decades, the competition has produced many memorable finals and dramatic matches decided in extra time or on penalties, contributing to its reputation as one of the most unpredictable competitions in Romanian football.

Most finals have historically been held in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"), particularly at the [Stadionul Național](/wiki/Stadionul_Na%C8%9Bional_(1953) "Stadionul Național (1953)") (formerly known as "23 August"). In recent decades the final has occasionally been hosted in other major cities across the country. During the construction of the [new Stadionul Național](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională"), the final was staged each year in a different city. In 2007, the final was held in [Timișoara](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara") at the [Dan Păltinișanu](/wiki/Stadionul_Dan_P%C4%83ltini%C8%99anu "Stadionul Dan Păltinișanu") stadium, only the second time the event had taken place outside Bucharest after [Brașov](/wiki/Bra%C8%99ov "Brașov") hosted the 1989 final. In the following years, finals were also staged in cities such as [Piatra Neamț](/wiki/Piatra_Neam%C8%9B "Piatra Neamț"), [Târgu Jiu](/wiki/T%C3%A2rgu_Jiu "Târgu Jiu"), [Iași](/wiki/Ia%C8%99i "Iași") and again in Brașov, before several editions returned to Bucharest following the opening of the [Arena Națională](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională").

Historically, the competition has been dominated by clubs from Bucharest. According to the official records of the [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation"), the most successful club in the history of the competition is [Steaua București](/wiki/CSA_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti_(football) "CSA Steaua București (football)") with 23 trophies, followed by [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București") and [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") with 13 trophies each. Court decisions in Romania regarding the legal dispute between [CSA Steaua București](/wiki/CSA_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti_(football) "CSA Steaua București (football)") and [FCSB](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB") established that the historical honours of Steaua București up to 1998 belong to the CSA Steaua sports club. As a result, the Romanian Cup trophies won by the club before that date are officially attributed to Steaua București in FRF records.

One of the most remarkable achievements in the competition’s history belongs to [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București"), which won the Romanian Cup six consecutive times between 1937 and 1942, a record that still stands today.

## Sponsorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Sponsorship")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/1d/Cupa_Romaniei.png/250px-Cupa_Romaniei.png)](/wiki/File:Cupa_Romaniei.png)

Former logo between 2006 and 2016

On 22 July 2005, FRF and [Samsung Electronics](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics") signed a one-year sponsorship deal. The name of the competition was changed to *Cupa României Samsung*.[[1]](#cite_note-2)

On 9 October 2006, FRF and [Ursus Breweries](/wiki/Ursus_Breweries "Ursus Breweries") (part of the [SABMiller](/wiki/SABMiller "SABMiller") group) signed a sponsorship agreement for the next three seasons. Ursus Breweries changed the name of the competition to *Cupa României Timișoreana*, after the [Timișoreana beer](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oreana "Timișoreana") brand.[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-4)

On 16 May 2016, FRF announced the rebranding of competition and the signing of contracts with new sponsors such as [Kaufland](/wiki/Kaufland "Kaufland"), [UPC Broadband](/wiki/UPC_Broadband "UPC Broadband") and [Stanleybet](/wiki/Stanleybet_International "Stanleybet International").[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6)

On 20 October 2017, FRF announced that the new main sponsor of the competition is the betting company Casa Pariurilor.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

## Competition format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Competition format")]

The competition has undergone minor changes in format over the years. The following format came in use in the [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2009–10 Cupa României") season. The main differences between the current system and the last one are the dates at which rounds take place, and the two-legged format of the semifinals.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

### County phase

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: County phase")]

The competition at this phase is organized by the county football associations. Forty-two teams (one from each county) advance to the next phase.

### National phase

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: National phase")]

The competition at this phase is organized by the [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation") (FRF). For the first five rounds, teams are paired using geographical criteria in order to avoid long travel distances. The teams from a lower division or with a lower ranking in the last league season host the games.

* First round – 140 teams (42 teams qualified from the county phase and 98 [Liga III](/wiki/Liga_III "Liga III") teams)
* Second round – 80 teams (70 winners from the first round and the remaining 10 Liga III teams)
* Third round – 40 teams (winners from the second round)
* Fourth round – 56 teams (20 winners from the third round and all 36 [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II") teams)
* Fifth round – 28 teams (winners from the fourth round)
* Round of 32 – (14 winners from the fifth round and all 18 [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") teams)

:   Starting with this round a seeding system is used for the draw, as follows:

* + Pot A: Teams 1–6 from last season's Liga I final table (6 teams)
  + Pot B: The remaining Liga I teams (12 teams)
  + Pot C: Teams from the lower divisions (14 teams)

:   Teams from pot A are paired with teams from pot C, then the eight remaining pot C teams are paired with pot B teams, with the lower league clubs hosting the games. The four remaining pot B teams will play each other, with the host club determined by means of a draw.

* Round of 16 (winners from the Round of 32)
* Quarterfinals
* Semifinals
* Final

Every year, based on the national and international football calendar, FRF's executive committee may choose a two-leg or one-leg system for the round of 16, quarterfinals and semifinals.[[8]](#cite_note-9) Games at these stages are, when played using a one-leg system, hosted by a neutral venue. The final is held at a pre-established venue, normally in Bucharest.
